author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
Victor Utterly Unable to Explain How the Sinless Soul Deserved to Be Made Sinful.
originalBook: Treatise on the Soul and Its Origin
title: Victor Utterly Unable to Explain How the Sinless Soul Deserved to Be Made Sinful.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But what does he mean by that, which in his introduction he says has befallen him?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For previous to proposing that question of his, and as introducing it, he affirms: “There are other opprobrious expressions underlying the querulous murmurings of those who rail at us; and, shaken about as in a hurricane, we are again and again dashed amongst enormous rocks.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, if I were to express myself about him in this style, he would probably be angry.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: The words are his; and after premising them, he propounded his question, by way of showing us the very rocks against which he struck and was wrecked.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For to such lengths was he carried, and against such frightful reefs was he borne, drifted, and struck, that his escape was a perfect impossibility without a retreat—a correction, in short, of what he had said; since he was unable to show by what desert the soul was made sinful; though he was not afraid to say, that previous to any sin of its own it had deserved to become sinful.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, who deserves, without committing any sin, so immense a punishment as to be conceived in the sin of another, before leaving his mother’s womb, and then to be no longer free from sin?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But from this punishment the free grace of God delivers the souls of such infants as are regenerated in Christ, with no previous merits of their own—otherwise grace is no grace.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: With regard, then, to this person, who is so vastly intelligent, and who in the great depth of his wisdom is displeased at our hesitation, which, if not well informed, is at all events circumspect, let him tell us, if he can, what the merit was which brought the soul into such a punishment, from which grace delivers it without any merit.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Let him speak, and, if he can, defend his assertion with some show of reason.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: I would not, indeed, require so much of him, if he had not himself declared that the soul deserved to become sinful.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Let him tell us what the desert was—whether good desert or evil?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If good, how could well-deserving lead to evil?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If evil, whence could arise any ill desert previous to the commission of any sin?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: I have also to remark, that if there be a good desert, then the liberation of the soul would not be of free grace, but it would be due to the previous merit, and thus “grace would be no more grace.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If there be, however, an evil desert, then I ask what it is.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Is it true that the soul has come into the flesh; and that it would not have so come unless He in whom there is no sin had Himself sent it?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Never, therefore, except by floundering worse and worse, will he contrive to set up this view of his, in which he predicates of the soul that it deserved to be sinful.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: In the case of those infants, too, in whose baptism original sin is washed away, he found something to say after a fashion,—to the effect, that being involved in the sin of another could not possibly have been detrimental to them, predestinated as they were to eternal life in the foreknowledge of God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: This might admit of a tolerably good sense, if he had not entangled himself in that formula of his, in which he asserts that the soul deserved to be sinful: from this difficulty he can only extricate himself by revoking his words, with regret at having expressed them.
